mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers all four laws of thermodynamics: the zeroth law establishing temperature (A.4.b), the first law on conservation of energy (A.2), the second law on entropy and directionality (A.3), and the third law concerning absolute zero (A.6). Examines heat as energy transfer (A.4.c), free energy (A.5), the Carnot cycle (A.5.a), and thermodynamic behavior of gases (A.5.d).

Entropy as a fundamental concept encompassing the second law (A.3), the Carnot cycle (A.5.a), heat and its relation to entropy, work, and change of energy (A.4.c.v), and statistical thermodynamics through the reinterpretation of entropy as a measure of ignorance connecting microscopic structure to macroscopic laws (A.9).

Thermodynamics including entropy and absolute zero (A.2, A.3), statistical thermodynamics (A.9), states of matter and phase transitions (A.5.c), equations of state (A.5.d, B.2), and the thermodynamic approach to gases (B.2).
Energy transfer and entropy as foundational chemistry principles (A), and heat energy concepts in the context of industrial applications including fuels and power generation (A.4.c).

Behavior of liquids and gases in motion and at rest (B, C), fluid dynamics and hydrodynamics (H.3), transport properties of fluids (C.3.a), the continuum hypothesis applied to flow behaviors (H), and fundamental physical principles governing both liquid and gas states (C.1).

States of matter including gases (B), liquids (C), plasmas (F), and exotic quantum matter (G), covering fundamental physics from elementary particles through bulk states and the forces governing different forms of matter.

Polymers, colloids, liquid crystals, and foams as colloidal dispersions (E.4), Brownian motion linking molecular-scale thermal motion to macroscopic behavior relevant to kinetic molecular theory (H.1), viscosity and adhesion as transport and surface properties (C.3.a, E.1), and surface tension phenomena (E.1).

Physical and chemical properties across three states: liquid (C), ice relating to solid-state, and steam as a gas (B). Molecular structure governing anomalous behaviors (C.1), properties of water as a liquid (C.3), solutions and solubility involving water as a solvent (D), surface tension (E.1), and low-temperature properties relating to ice (G.1).

Zero electrical resistance below critical temperatures as a special phenomenon at very low temperatures (G.1.b), effects of low temperature on conductivity and magnetic properties (G.1.a), and methods for obtaining low temperatures (G.1.c). BCS theory and Cooper pairing illustrate quantum mechanical phenomena in condensed matter at extreme conditions.

Phase transitions and symmetry breaking relating to phase changes and equilibria (A.5.c), liquid crystals and colloids as colloidal systems (E.4), superfluids and superconductors as special phenomena at very low temperatures (G.1.b), and effects of temperature on physical properties at extreme conditions (G.1).
Molecular interactions including hydrogen bonds relevant to solvent and solute interactions (D.3), and solutions of polymers and proteins as reversible colloidal systems (E.4.b).
Physics of sound covering wave behavior and propagation directly relevant to acoustic properties of liquids including sound wave propagation in liquid media (C.3.b).
Nuclear fission and fusion technologies for power production as applications of plasmas and high-energy states (F.8).
Solar processes driven by magnetic and thermal dynamics involving plasma states (F), and the Sun as a natural plasma system existing in the extraterrestrial medium (F.9).

Stellar interiors as plasma states existing in nature including in stars (F.9), and nuclear fusion processes at extreme temperatures relevant to special properties of matter at high temperatures (G.2).

Overview of thermodynamics as fundamental physics (A, A.2, A.3), treatment of states of matter including gases (B) and condensed phases (C), and coverage spanning classical mechanics through quantum theory relevant to understanding matter at all scales.
Entropy in information theory and Maxwell's demon in thermodynamics connecting information-theoretic entropy to thermodynamic entropy and statistical mechanics (A.9).
Historical development of chemistry including pneumatic chemistry and theories of heat from phlogiston through caloric theory to kinetic molecular theory (A.4.c.ii).
